# A05G Release (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Mar 4, 202611:59 PM EST

A **group** submission on Gradescope.

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

| Note: No late submissions for this assignment\! We must submit the PCBs on time to be manufactured in time for us to bring up, debug, and program them. |
| :---- |

# Learning Outcomes

* Learn how to generate files that can be interpreted by manufacturing facilities.  
* See how 3D printing can give you a better understanding of your circuit board size and features \- and how you might integrate a casework around the PCBA.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 

# 0\. Order remaining off-board components

There are many components on your PCBA that were depopulated: **connectors, resistors, all through-hole components, etc**. It is now time for you to order these components so you may solder them onto your board later in the semester. **If you fail to order these parts, you will not have them for your final project assembly.** 

**If your off-board components total more than <!-- $60 -->, talk with your account manager.**

**Dev Board & Non-dev-board Stuff Specification**  
If you plan to use **off-board development boards** for your final demo, you must order them as part of this assignment.

* **Example:** If you are using a screen that connects to your PCB via connectors and it is **not** your most risky item, you must place the order now. This also includes non-dev-board stuff like motors.

* **Exception:** If you plan to mount an IMU **directly on your PCB** and it is **not** classified as your most risky item, you do **not** need to order it. However, if you later find that the onboard sensor does not function properly, you may request a dev board for that sensor by contacting the teaching team.

**Submission:** Order remaining components using this form: [https://forms.gle/t21AdA35igJYsziRA](https://forms.gle/t21AdA35igJYsziRA) 

# 

# 1\. PCB Finishing Touches

Add the finishing touches to your PCB\!

1. Implement feedback from the teaching team.  
2. Add a version number for your PCB. (Hint: Usually, for our first PCBA release, we call it  **Version 1** / **V1**. This helps us determine the difference in release versions.)  
3. Add a top or bottom overlay / silkscreen fill region for your PCBA serial numbering.  
   ![][image1]  
4. Add any cosmetic/help text on the silkscreen you deem necessary.   
   1. It’s a great idea to label the pins on each connector with their net labels\!  
5. Add any solder gang masks if needed as seen in class.  
6. Add any PCB mounting holes for your final casework / prototype.  
7. Review the [PCB Design Review Checklist ESE5160 S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ipyIuc_Ly_mjgZjhHJGv7Ep6g4OOlyAYM76pn7uFIx8/edit?tab=t.0) one more time\!  
8. Must pass Project Validation and Design Rule Check.  
9. In your PCB variant, Do Not Populate (DNP) all [through hole components](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Through-hole_technology).  
   1. The SD card and microUSB connector **must remain populated\!** They are considered surface mount.

| *You must visit your account manager once you done with output job file and before placing the order PCBA* |
| :---: |

## PCB Documentation

Complete the Draftsman files as seen in class (manufacturing and assembly). Create and update your OutputJob file to generate the required manufacturing and design files. Don’t forget to select your PCB variant.

**Note: Ensure the text blocks of the schematics and Draftsman documents are filled with your information (bottom left corner of document). Some of these should be filled automatically.**

## PCB Manufacturing Release

Make a manufacturing release in Altium 365\. Released files leverage the OutputJob files and variants in your project to create the manufacturing package for the PCBA house. These releases are also an excellent way to internally track projects and their files.

Use the best practices from the lectures and [Altium’s Project Releaser](https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/board-design-release#!the-project-releaser) documentation to generate your manufacturing release. Ensure you use descriptive comments in your release notes and commits\!

After it’s been released, double check that it exists in the A365 web view. Try downloading the release \- does it have all of the files required? (Hint: if not, you need to fix that\!)  
![][image2]

It’s good practice to test check your output Gerber files using a secondary program. You can use [gerbv](http://gerbv.geda-project.org/) to review your Gerber files to ensure that everything was correctly output.

**(1.1)** On Gradescope, add a link to your A365 project after committing all your work for the PCB layout.

* Create Draftsman drawings for Fabrication and Assembly  
* Ensure you’ve created a manufacturing release using Altium’s Project Releaser. The project release must appear in the project history timeline.

| Your team will be submitting your manufacturing files to JLCPCB for manufacture after your account manager approves the design. |
| :---- |

# Critical Notes

This is it\! Whatever you submit will be sent to manufacturing as-is. Please double and triple check that every piece of feedback given to you has been implemented, that everything seems ok, and that there are no rules and violations left to fix.

The teaching team makes our best effort to check your work as thoroughly as possible. It is still possible that an issue might slip through review. **Your team remains the expert in your design, and we recommend that you scrutinize your own work before submitting.**

A reality of hardware manufacturing is that once a file is sent to manufacturing, any issues that remain will get manufactured. It is vital in hardware design to be very thorough and check and recheck everything in order to reduce any risk of issues getting to manufacturing to zero.

There is a lot of complexity in hardware design. It is easy for issues to slip past these checks, even though multiple eyes have reviewed the design. If any issues arise and are not caught, they can be fixed by doing board rework. We will have a class on this subject. This is one reason we ask you to use larger passive components, break out pins to test points, and route extra MCU pins.

# 3D Printed PCBA

Get an idea of how your PCBA will look and feel\! Export your PCBA to a STEP file and [submit it to the RPL](https://meamlabs.seas.upenn.edu/rapid-prototyping-lab/makerfleet/) for printing. You will likely not have the 3D print before this assignment is due, so please show proof that you’ve submitted it for printing (such as an email chain with the RPL team) and present your 3D print to me when you receive it.

Export your PCBA as a STEP file from Altium (check **Export As Single Part** and leave all other settings the same as the image below). Open the STEP file in SolidWorks (do **not** run **Import Diagnostics** on the part) and save it as an STL file. Email the STL file to [meamrpl@seas.upenn.edu](mailto:meamrpl@seas.upenn.edu) and ask them to print the PCBA with the following settings:

* 0.2mm layer height  
* 2 perimeters  
* 10% infill  
* Supports

![][image3]![][image4]  
![][image5]![][image6]

No online submission required for this section \- bring to class after spring break and show to Nick or your Account Manager.

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
